main_dirt.bias_notes: A standard 1-mile dirt oval with a 960-foot home stretch and chutes for 6-furlong and 1 1/4-mile races. The track relies entirely on dirt racing, and the surface can frequently develop a strong inside/speed bias, particularly during the dry summer months. Because the corners are relatively standard, early positioning is critical.
main_dirt.post_bias: In the standard 5f to 6f sprint dashes, inside and middle posts (1-4) hold a significant statistical advantage. Wide draws are punished unless the horse possesses enough explosive early speed to cross over before the first turn. In two-turn route races, saving ground on the inside is highly preferred.

handicapping_angles.the_downstate_shipper: Finger Lakes is the primary landing spot for horses dropping out of the ultra-competitive 'downstate' NYRA circuit (Aqueduct, Belmont, Saratoga). Aggressively upgrade horses shipping into Finger Lakes from NYRA tracks, as they typically hold a massive inherent class edge over the local stalwarts.
handicapping_angles.inside_speed_dominance: Particularly in sprints, pure early speed ('E' types) drawn on the inside rail are incredibly potent. If an inside-drawn horse projects to be the lone speed on paper, they are extremely difficult to run down.
handicapping_angles.weather_dependent_surface: As a track operating in upstate New York from spring through late autumn, the sandy loam surface can drastically change with precipitation. Monitor early races on wet days; if the rail becomes deep and tiring (a 'dead rail'), immediately upgrade outside stalkers.
